title: Understanding Usability of VR Pointing Methods with a Handheld-style HMD for Onsite Exhibitions
abstract: Handheld-style head-mounted displays (HMDs) are becoming increasingly popular as a convenient option for onsite exhibitions. However, they lack established practices for basic interactions, particularly pointing methods. Through our formative study involving practitioners, we discovered that controllers and hand gestures are the primary pointing methods being utilized. Building upon these findings, we conducted a usability study to explore seven different pointing methods, incorporating insights from the formative study and current virtual reality (VR) practices. The results showed that while controllers remain a viable option, hand gestures are not recommended. Notably, dwell time-based methods, which are not fast and are not commonly recognized by practitioners, demonstrate high usability and user confidence, particularly for inexperienced VR users.We recommend the use of dwell-based methods for onsite exhibition contexts. This research provides insights for the adoption of handheld-style HMDs, laying the groundwork for improving user interaction in exhibition environments, thereby potentially enhancing visitor experiences.

publication.title: Understanding Usability of VR Pointing Methods with a Handheld-style HMD for Onsite Exhibitions
publication.authors: Yuki Abe*, ***Kan Kusakabe****, Myungguen Choi*, Daisuke Sakamoto, Tetsuo Ono. *Joint first authors.

publication.publisher: ACM CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI 2025), ACM, 2025. **Hornable Mention Award (Acceptance rate 5%)**.

title: RingSense: Exploring User-defined Gestures for Phone Ring Holders
abstract: We present RingSense, a phone ring-holder-style device equipped with eight pressure sensors for back-of-device (BoD) interaction on smartphones. The phone ring holder is a ring-type smartphone accessory attached to the back of the smartphone, and RingSense can be easily affixed to the smartphone, similar to a standard smartphone accessory. RingSense serves as both a smartphone input device and a safety accessory for fall prevention. To develop RingSense, we first conducted a gesture elicitation study and created a user-defined gesture set for the phone ring holder. Next, we prototyped RingSense using 8 pressure sensors and a machine-learning-based gesture classification system. We then evaluated our system using the gesture data from 10 participants. Consequently, our system achieved 92.7% accuracy for within-user validation and showed the feasibility of gesture classification by our approach. Finally, we demonstrated some potential applications of RingSense.

publication.title: RingSense: Exploring User-defined Gestures for Phone Ring Holders

publication.publisher: Transactions of the Information Processing Society of Japan, Vol. 66, No. 2

publication.title: Design and Implementation of a Phone-Ring-Type Device for Back-of-Device Gesture Interaction (in Japanese).
publication.publisher: The 30th Workshop on Interactive Systems and Software (WISS’22), Japan Society for Software Science and Technology, pp.8-14, December 2022. (Long-press, acceptance rate: 12.5%).

title: BodSockets: Enabling Back-of-Device Interaction on Smartphones with Socket-based Interface
abstract: We present BodSockets, an interface utilizing the form factor of collapsible phone grips, which is a smartphone accessory. BodSockets enables Back-of-Device interaction on smartphones through gestural input. We clarified the usage patterns of oﬀ-the-shelf collapsible phone grips through a crowdsourced study (N=300). Based on these findings, we prototyped BodSockets with 56 gestures categorized into IMU-based, touchscreen-based, and combined gestures. Our gesture recognition system achieved an accuracy of 91.5% for IMU-based, 91.0% for touchscreen-based, and 90.1% for combined-based gestures, respectively. Through a workshop, four promising use cases of BodSockets were revealed: (1) improving one-handed interaction; (2) reducing repetitive operations; (3) enabling error-free smartphone operation by utilizing both front and back inputs; and (4) enhancing intuitiveness and physical engagement through elastic feedback. Finally, we discuss the implications of our findings for the design of BodSockets-based interfaces.

publication.title: BodSockets: Enabling Back-of-Device Interaction on Smartphones with Socket-based Interface
publication.authors: ***Kan Kusakabe***, Kaori Ikematsu, Daisuke Sakamoto, Tetsuo Ono.

title: Game-2-X: A System Connecting Different Types of Gameplay (in Japanese).
abstract: Communication through gameplay can enhance trust between parents and children and facilitate ice-breaking among strangers. However, opportunities for such interactions may be missed when not all participants are familiar with the same game's rules. To address this issue, we propose ''Game-2-X,'' a system that dynamically adjusts the difficulty of one game based on players’ activities in a different type of game environment. By using Game-2-X, each user can continue playing a game they are adept at while still connecting to another player's distinct game—enabling cross-game communication that was previously difficult. As an initial implementation, we focused on board games, demonstrating a working prototype that connects specific board games in real-time.

publication.title: Game-2-X: A System Connecting Different Types of Gameplay (in Japanese).
publication.authors: ***Kan Kusakabe****, Yuki Abe*, Daisuke Sakamoto, Tetsuo Ono. *Joint first author.
publication.publisher: The 31st Workshop on Interactive Systems and Software (WISS’23), Japan Society for Software Science and Technology, Demonstration. December 2023.
